subtitle: Journal-club style ICU update: PICO, key results, appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English.

script.heading: Intro
sentences: Welcome to Critical Care Daily for June 29, 2026.
Today we will review 3 recent ICU-relevant publications.
The format is quick listen first, then PICO, key results, critical appraisal, bedside impact, and medical English notes.
Use this as a morning triage tool, then open the source link for full-text appraisal before changing practice.

script.heading: Story 1 - Quetiapine Versus Haloperidol for the Treatment of Delirium in
sentences: Story 1 is about Quetiapine Versus Haloperidol for the Treatment of Delirium in Hospitalized Adults: A Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials with Trial Sequential Analysis..
This is a systematic review / meta-analysis from General hospital psychiatry.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in adults, how should we interpret Quetiapine compared with Haloperidol for the Treatment of Delirium in Hospitalized Adults: A Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials with Trial Sequential Analysis?
Four RCTs comprising 292 patients were included.

No significant difference was found in delirium severity reduction (MD, -1.99; 95% CI, -4.70 to 0.72; p = 0.15; I2 = 73.6%), ICU LOS (MD, -0.84 days; 95% CI, -2.30 to 0.62), hospital LOS (MD, -0.06 days; 95% CI, -2.32 to 2.19), or mortality (RR, 0.68; 95% CI, 0.35 to 1.34).
Quetiapine showed non-significant trends toward fewer EPS (RR, 0.14; 95% CI, 0.02 to 1.06) and increased sleep duration (MD, 1.59 h; 95% CI, -0.45 to 3.63).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Worth discussing in journal club; consider practice change only after checking absolute effects, harms, and applicability.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 2 - Crossover effect: causal machine learning reveals opposing mor
sentences: Story 2 is about Crossover effect: causal machine learning reveals opposing mortality responses to mean arterial pressure targets among phenotypically distinct hypertensive patients with septic shock..
This is a recent research article from Internal medicine journal.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in patients with septic shock from the Medical Information Mart for Intensive Care IV database (version 3, how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?

In the Strong Benefit group (Decompensated Phenotype) - characterised by tachycardia, hyperlactatemia and renal dysfunction - the high MAP target dramatically reduced 28-day mortality from 62.6% to 22.9% (absolute risk reduction = 39.7%, P < 0.001).
Conversely, in the Harm group (Frail Phenotype) - characterised by older age and lower heart rate - the high target increased mortality from 14.7% to 33.9% (ARR = -19.2%, P < 0.001).
We observed marked HTE (ITE range: -0.54 to +0.86).
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Awareness item: read the full source before applying it at the bedside.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Story 3 - MicroRNAs in sepsis diagnosis: A systematic review and meta-an
sentences: Story 3 is about MicroRNAs in sepsis diagnosis: A systematic review and meta-analysis toward evidence-based biomarker development..
This is a systematic review / meta-analysis from Journal of microbiology, immunology, and infection = Wei mian yu gan ran za zhi.

The clinical question is best framed as follows: in Critically ill or ICU-relevant patients described in the abstract; confirm eligibility criteria in the full text., how should we interpret Exposure or intervention stated in the title/objective. compared with Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.?
Pooled sensitivity was 0.79 (95% CI: 0.76-0.82) and specificity was 0.82 (95% CI: 0.78-0.85), with an AUROC of 0.87 (95% CI: 0.84-0.90).
Substantial heterogeneity was observed (sensitivity I2 = 92.4%; specificity I2 = 89.0%).

In exploratory functional subgroup analyses, IL-1β-associated miRNAs demonstrated higher overall diagnostic accuracy (AUROC 0.90, 95% CI: 0.87-0.92), while endothelial dysfunction-related miRNAs showed comparatively higher specificity.
For critical appraisal, focus on whether the design supports a causal conclusion and whether the patients resemble your ICU population.
The bedside implication is this: Worth discussing in journal club; consider practice change only after checking absolute effects, harms, and applicability.
Useful English phrase: these findings should be interpreted cautiously because the details of design and applicability matter.

script.heading: Outro
sentences: That wraps up today's journal club style update.
Before applying any finding, check the full source, the absolute effect size, and your local ICU context.
For English practice, repeat one academic phrase from each article out loud.

articles.title: Quetiapine Versus Haloperidol for the Treatment of Delirium in Hospitalized Adults: A Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials with Trial Sequential Analysis.
articles.summary: Four RCTs comprising 292 patients were included. No significant difference was found in delirium severity reduction (MD, -1.99; 95% CI, -4.70 to 0.72; p = 0.15; I2 = 73.6%), ICU LOS (MD, -0.84 days; 95% CI, -2.30 to 0.62), hospital LOS (MD, -0.06 days; 95% CI, -2.32 to 2.19), or mortality (RR, 0.68; 95% CI, 0.35 to 1.34). Quetiapine showed non-significant trends toward fewer EPS (RR, 0.14; 95% CI, 0.02 to 1.06) and increased sleep duration (MD, 1.59 h; 95% CI, -0.45 to 3.63). Quetiapine and haloperidol show no statistically significant differences in efficacy or safety for delirium management. To evaluate the comparative efficacy and safety of quetiapine versus haloperidol for delirium treatment in hospitalized adults.
articles.analysis: Worth discussing in journal club; consider practice change only after checking absolute effects, harms, and applicability.

pico.comparator: Haloperidol for the Treatment of Delirium in Hospitalized Adults: A Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials with Trial Sequential Analysis
pico.outcomes: Main outcomes are those reported in the abstract; prioritize patient-centered outcomes such as mortality, ventilation duration, ICU stay, and adverse events.

keyResults: Four RCTs comprising 292 patients were included.

No significant difference was found in delirium severity reduction (MD, -1.99; 95% CI, -4.70 to 0.72; p = 0.15; I2 = 73.6%), ICU LOS (MD, -0.84 days; 95% CI, -2.30 to 0.62), hospital LOS (MD, -0.06 days; 95% CI, -2.32 to 2.19), or mortality (RR, 0.68; 95% CI, 0.35 to 1.34).
Quetiapine showed non-significant trends toward fewer EPS (RR, 0.14; 95% CI, 0.02 to 1.06) and increased sleep duration (MD, 1.59 h; 95% CI, -0.45 to 3.63).
Quetiapine and haloperidol show no statistically significant differences in efficacy or safety for delirium management.
criticalAppraisal: High-yield synthesis: check heterogeneity, included study quality, and whether ICU patients match your setting.
Look for heterogeneity, small-study effects, and whether pooled outcomes are clinically comparable.
articles.practiceImpact: Worth discussing in journal club; consider practice change only after checking absolute effects, harms, and applicability.

phrases: The effect estimate favored ..., but the confidence interval ...
These findings should be interpreted cautiously because ...

articles.title: Crossover effect: causal machine learning reveals opposing mortality responses to mean arterial pressure targets among phenotypically distinct hypertensive patients with septic shock.
articles.summary: In the Strong Benefit group (Decompensated Phenotype) - characterised by tachycardia, hyperlactatemia and renal dysfunction - the high MAP target dramatically reduced 28-day mortality from 62.6% to 22.9% (absolute risk reduction = 39.7%, P < 0.001). Conversely, in the Harm group (Frail Phenotype) - characterised by older age and lower heart rate - the high target increased mortality from 14.7% to 33.9% (ARR = -19.2%, P < 0.001). To quantify the heterogeneity of treatment effects of higher versus standard MAP targets on 28-day mortality, to identify distinct clinical phenotypes that respond differentially to MAP targets, and to derive simple bedside decision rules to guide individualised haemodynamic management. Current guidelines recommend a uniform mean arterial pressure (MAP) target of ≥65 mmHg for septic shock. However, trials like SEPSISPAM found no mortality benefit from higher targets in hypertensive patients.
articles.analysis: Awareness item: read the full source before applying it at the bedside.

keyResults: In the Strong Benefit group (Decompensated Phenotype) - characterised by tachycardia, hyperlactatemia and renal dysfunction - the high MAP target dramatically reduced 28-day mortality from 62.6% to 22.9% (absolute risk reduction = 39.7%, P < 0.001).
Conversely, in the Harm group (Frail Phenotype) - characterised by older age and lower heart rate - the high target increased mortality from 14.7% to 33.9% (ARR = -19.2%, P < 0.001).
We observed marked HTE (ITE range: -0.54 to +0.86).
The CART model identified heart rate >105 bpm and creatinine >1.65 mg/dL as simple bedside criteria to identify patients likely to benefit from higher targets.
criticalAppraisal: Early evidence: useful for awareness, not enough alone to change practice.
articles.practiceImpact: Awareness item: read the full source before applying it at the bedside.

articles.title: MicroRNAs in sepsis diagnosis: A systematic review and meta-analysis toward evidence-based biomarker development.
articles.source: Journal of microbiology, immunology, and infection = Wei mian yu gan ran za zhi
articles.summary: Pooled sensitivity was 0.79 (95% CI: 0.76-0.82) and specificity was 0.82 (95% CI: 0.78-0.85), with an AUROC of 0.87 (95% CI: 0.84-0.90). Substantial heterogeneity was observed (sensitivity I2 = 92.4%; specificity I2 = 89.0%). In exploratory functional subgroup analyses, IL-1β-associated miRNAs demonstrated higher overall diagnostic accuracy (AUROC 0.90, 95% CI: 0.87-0.92), while endothelial dysfunction-related miRNAs showed comparatively higher specificity. Sepsis diagnosis remains challenging, and conventional biomarkers such as CRP and PCT show limited specificity. Circulating microRNAs (miRNAs) have been investigated as diagnostic biomarkers, but substantial methodological variability across studies has limited the interpretation of current evidence.
articles.analysis: Worth discussing in journal club; consider practice change only after checking absolute effects, harms, and applicability.

pico.comparator: Comparator or control group stated in the methods/results, if present.
pico.outcomes: Thirty-nine studies involving 59 distinct miRNAs were included. Pooled sensitivity was 0.79 (95% CI: 0.76-0.82) and specificity was 0.82 (95% CI: 0.78-0.85), with an AUROC of 0.87 (95% CI: 0.84-0.90). Substantial heterogeneity was observed (sensitivity I2 = 92.4%; specificity I2 = 89.0%). In exploratory functional subgroup analyses, IL-1β-associated...

keyResults: Pooled sensitivity was 0.79 (95% CI: 0.76-0.82) and specificity was 0.82 (95% CI: 0.78-0.85), with an AUROC of 0.87 (95% CI: 0.84-0.90).
Substantial heterogeneity was observed (sensitivity I2 = 92.4%; specificity I2 = 89.0%).

In exploratory functional subgroup analyses, IL-1β-associated miRNAs demonstrated higher overall diagnostic accuracy (AUROC 0.90, 95% CI: 0.87-0.92), while endothelial dysfunction-related miRNAs showed comparatively higher specificity.
Thirty-nine studies involving 59 distinct miRNAs were included.
criticalAppraisal: High-yield synthesis: check heterogeneity, included study quality, and whether ICU patients match your setting.
Look for heterogeneity, small-study effects, and whether pooled outcomes are clinically comparable.
Single-center data may not generalize well to different ICU resources or case-mix.
articles.practiceImpact: Worth discussing in journal club; consider practice change only after checking absolute effects, harms, and applicability.
